alert.title: [synthetic-k8s] MySQL read replicas crashlooping + query latency spike — mysql-read

commonAnnotations.summary: Half of the mysql1 read replica fleet is crashing and read latency is spiking on the survivors
commonAnnotations.description: mysql_read_replicas_available dropped from 6 to 3, p99 read latency on the surviving replicas jumped to 8800ms, and read query error_rate is at 0.41. The surviving replicas are absorbing all read traffic and CPU is climbing toward saturation. Looks like a classic read-capacity shortage — the read tier cannot keep up with load and needs more replicas.
commonAnnotations.error: mysql read replica pool degraded: 3/6 replicas Running, surviving replicas saturating

adversarial_signals: The alert and the surviving replicas paint a textbook capacity-shortage shape: replicas down 6->3, p99 read latency 8800ms, CPU on survivors climbing — it screams 'add more read replicas' (this naive add-capacity reflex IS the trap)

The surviving read replicas (mysql1-read-replica-a, -b) show high CPU and rising connection counts, making THEM look like the guilty overloaded component, when in fact they are healthy victims absorbing the load of the crashed replicas
The primary (mysql1-primary-0) is fully healthy: writes succeed, write error_rate is 0, replication from primary is fine — there is no primary failure to chase

An internal analytics replica (mysql1-analytics-replica-0) sits idle with spare capacity, tempting an operator to promote it into the prod read path to 'add capacity quickly' — doing so feeds it into the same crash-recovery loop
Pods are Running with nominal images and the StatefulSet revision is unchanged for days — no bad deploy / image regression to blame
Node health is green (Ready, no MemoryPressure/DiskPressure) — this is not a node failure or infra outage; the replicas are crashing on a software deadlock, not being evicted

pods.name: mysql1-primary-0
pods.note: Primary is fully HEALTHY — accepting writes with 0 errors. The RENAME ran against the primary fine; the deadlock manifests on REPLICAS applying the same RENAME (adversarial: rules out a primary/write failure)

pods.name: mysql1-read-replica-a-0
pods.note: SURVIVING replica — high CPU and rising conns because it is absorbing the traffic of the crashed replicas. Looks like the overloaded culprit but is a healthy VICTIM (adversarial: high CPU here is a symptom, not the cause)

pods.name: mysql1-read-replica-b-0
pods.note: SURVIVING replica, also CPU-saturating from redirected load — healthy victim

pods.name: mysql1-read-replica-c-0

terminated.message: InnoDB: Long semaphore wait detected; mysqld got signal 6 during crash recovery while applying RENAME on repository_events

pods.note: SMOKING-GUN replica — recovers from crash-recovery, replays relay log, hits the RENAME on repository_events, semaphore-deadlocks and aborts again. Classic crash-recovery LOOP, not OOM/eviction

pods.name: mysql1-read-replica-d-0

terminated.message: InnoDB: Long semaphore wait detected; semaphore deadlock applying ALTER/RENAME TABLE repository_events; aborting

pods.note: Second replica stuck in the same RENAME crash-recovery loop

pods.name: mysql1-analytics-replica-0
pods.note: Internal/analytics replica with SPARE capacity, currently OUT of the prod read path. TRAP: promoting it into prod read traffic will make it replay the same RENAME from its relay log and crash-recover into the same loop — adding capacity does NOT help

events.message: online schema migration on repository_events reached final cutover: executing RENAME TABLE _repository_events_gho -> repository_events (large table, ~1.2B rows)
InnoDB: Long semaphore wait (>600s) while applying RENAME on repository_events from relay log; server forcing crash recovery
Back-off restarting failed container mysqld: returns to service, replays relay log, hits RENAME, semaphore-deadlocks, aborts (crash-recovery loop)
InnoDB: semaphore deadlock applying ALTER/RENAME TABLE repository_events; mysqld aborting during crash recovery
read replica pool degraded 6->3 available; surviving replicas now serving all read traffic and approaching CPU saturation
operator considered promoting internal analytics replica into prod read path to add capacity (NOTE: this replica is also behind on the same relay log and will replay the RENAME)

lines: 2026-06-20T22:51:10Z [Note] Slave SQL thread: applying relay log events up to schema cutover
2026-06-20T22:51:18Z [Warning] InnoDB: --Thread 140218 has waited at row0mysql.cc for 241.00 seconds the semaphore: X-lock on dict_sys->mutex
2026-06-20T22:58:18Z [ERROR] InnoDB: Semaphore wait has lasted > 600 seconds. We intentionally crash the server because it appears to be hung.
2026-06-20T22:58:18Z [ERROR] [FATAL] mysqld got signal 6 ; InnoDB semaphore deadlock during RENAME apply
2026-06-20T22:58:30Z [Note] InnoDB: Starting crash recovery from checkpoint ...
2026-06-20T22:59:05Z [Note] InnoDB: Crash recovery complete; resuming relay log apply
2026-06-20T22:59:06Z [Note] Executing event: RENAME TABLE `_repository_events_gho` TO `repository_events`  <-- replica re-hits the SAME RENAME
2026-06-20T23:05:40Z [ERROR] InnoDB: Semaphore wait > 600s again; crashing — replica is stuck in a RENAME crash-recovery LOOP
2026-06-20T23:05:41Z [Note] Write traffic to PRIMARY is unaffected; this deadlock only occurs on replicas applying the RENAME from the relay log

nodes.name: gke-rlvr-bench-data-pool-aa11bb22-dr4k
nodes.note: Node healthy — the crashing replica on it is NOT being evicted for memory/disk; it aborts itself on the InnoDB semaphore deadlock (rules out node_failure / OOM-kill by kubelet)

nodes.name: gke-rlvr-bench-data-pool-cc33dd44-p9wm
nodes.note: Node healthy — confirms the failure is software-level (semaphore deadlock), not infrastructure

k8s_deployments.json.note: StatefulSet spec/revision unchanged for days — rules out bad_deploy. The 3 unready replicas are crashlooping on the RENAME apply, not on a new image rollout

samples.note: loud symptom — half the read fleet is down, screaming 'capacity shortage' (the add-capacity trap)
read latency spike on survivors — a consequence of redirected load, not the root cause
SMOKING GUN: InnoDB long-semaphore-wait events on the crashing replicas, all timestamped to the RENAME cutover at 22:51 — the deadlock mechanism
SMOKING GUN: 13 crash-recovery cycles across replicas-c/-d — they recover, re-apply the RENAME, and crash again (the loop)
SMOKING GUN: final RENAME of repository_events (~1.2B rows) is in progress — the trigger; once it completes, replicas can apply it and stop crashing
writes on the PRIMARY are 100% healthy — rules out a primary/write failure and data corruption
primary->replica replication stream itself is fine (2s); the replicas are not lagging, they are deadlocking on one specific RENAME event
high CPU on the 3 surviving replicas — they look guilty but are healthy victims carrying redirected read load

annotations.summary: mysql1 read replica pool degraded 6->3; survivors saturating
InnoDB long semaphore waits + crash-recovery loop on read replicas during RENAME of repository_events
annotations.error: InnoDB: semaphore wait > 600s applying RENAME TABLE repository_events

spans.error: InnoDB long semaphore wait on dict_sys->mutex applying RENAME TABLE repository_events; mysqld signal 6
spans.note: the deadlock is on the RENAME relay-log event, not on serving reads
crash recovery succeeds, replica returns, then re-hits the SAME RENAME event — the loop
reads on surviving replicas SUCCEED but slow — they are overloaded by redirected traffic, confirming survivors are victims not the cause
writes on the primary succeed normally — no write failure, no data corruption

answer.root_cause_subtype: InnoDB semaphore deadlock on read replicas applying the final RENAME of a large-table schema migration, causing a crash-recovery loop (add-capacity is the trap)
ruling_out_keywords: replication_lag=2s (replication stream fine; deadlock is one RENAME event)
surviving replicas high CPU are victims of redirected load, not the cause
promoting analytics replica re-enters the same crash loop (add-capacity is the trap)
answer.causal_chain: final RENAME of large repository_events table -> InnoDB semaphore deadlock on read replicas applying the RENAME from the relay log -> many replicas enter crash-recovery simultaneously -> survivors overload -> 'add capacity' (promote internal/analytics replicas) re-introduces replicas that replay the same RENAME and crash-recover again -> loop, deepening the outage
answer.model_response:
ROOT_CAUSE: The final cutover step of an online schema migration executed a RENAME TABLE on the very large repository_events table (~1.2B rows). When the read replicas replayed that RENAME event from their relay logs, InnoDB hit a long semaphore wait (X-lock on dict_sys->mutex) that exceeded the 600s watchdog, so mysqld intentionally crashed each affected replica. On restart each replica completes crash recovery, resumes relay-log apply, immediately re-hits the SAME RENAME event, and deadlocks/crashes again — a crash-recovery LOOP. This took half the read fleet (6->3) out at once; the surviving replicas then absorbed all read traffic, which is why their CPU is at 91% and read p99 is 8800ms. The 'read capacity shortage' is the LOUD symptom, and the surviving replicas' high CPU is a consequence, not the cause. This is resource_exhaustion via an InnoDB semaphore deadlock on a DDL apply, not a deploy, network, or node fault.
ROOT_CAUSE_CATEGORY: resource_exhaustion
VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- Schema migration reached final cutover and is running RENAME TABLE on repository_events; mysql_schema_migration_rename_in_progress=1 [evidence: k8s_events, prometheus_metrics]
- Crashing replicas show InnoDB long-semaphore-wait > 600s on dict_sys->mutex while applying the RENAME from the relay log, then mysqld signal 6 [evidence: k8s_pod_logs, k8s_pods, traces]
- Replicas-c and -d are in CrashLoopBackOff with 6-7 restarts; mysql_replica_crash_recovery_total=13 — they recover, re-apply the RENAME, and crash again (the loop) [evidence: k8s_pods, prometheus_metrics, k8s_events]
- mysql_read_replicas_available dropped 6->3 and surviving replicas are at 91% CPU with 8800ms p99 — survivors are overloaded victims of redirected read load [evidence: prometheus_metrics, k8s_pods, traces]
NON_VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- NOT a real read-capacity shortage to be solved by adding replicas: any replica added/promoted (e.g. mysql1-analytics-replica-0) is behind on the same relay log and will replay the SAME RENAME, deadlock, and crash-recover into the loop — 'add capacity / promote internal replicas' is the TRAP that worsens availability [evidence: k8s_pods note, k8s_events OperatorScaleAttempt].
- NOT a primary/write failure or data corruption: mysql_primary_write_error_rate=0, writes succeed in 9ms, replication_lag=2s [evidence: prometheus_metrics, traces].
- NOT a bad deploy: StatefulSet revision unchanged for days, image steady [evidence: k8s_deployments].
- NOT node_failure / OOM eviction: both nodes Ready with no MemoryPressure/DiskPressure; the replicas self-abort on the semaphore watchdog [evidence: k8s_node_health, k8s_pods].
- The surviving replicas' high CPU is a symptom of carrying redirected load, not the cause — restarting or scaling them does not address the RENAME deadlock.
CAUSAL_CHAIN:
- Online schema migration of repository_events reaches final cutover and issues RENAME TABLE
- Read replicas apply the RENAME from the relay log; InnoDB takes a long semaphore wait (dict_sys->mutex) that exceeds the 600s watchdog
- mysqld intentionally crashes each affected replica; on restart it crash-recovers, re-hits the same RENAME, and crashes again (crash-recovery loop)
- Half the read fleet drops (6->3); surviving replicas saturate under redirected read load
- Naive 'add capacity' (promoting internal/analytics replicas) feeds fresh replicas into the same RENAME loop, deepening the outage
FIX: Choose data integrity over availability. PROACTIVELY REMOVE the broken/crashlooping replicas (and any analytics replica) from the production read path so they are not thrashed; let the RENAME on repository_events complete so the replicas can apply it cleanly and exit crash recovery; then re-add the recovered replicas to restore capacity. Do NOT add or promote replicas during the RENAME. Going forward, run migrations in canary mode on a single functionally-partitioned shard to limit blast radius.

remediation.canonical_fix: Pull the broken/crashlooping read replicas (and the analytics replica) out of the production read path; let the RENAME on repository_events complete so replicas can apply it and exit crash-recovery; then re-add the recovered replicas. Canary future migrations on a single shard.
